questions.text: What is the primary role of an 'Appraiser' in the context of insurance?
options: To sell policies to high-net-worth individuals
To determine the value of a property or life risk during underwriting
To investigate fraud after a claim
To manage the insurance company's marketing team
questions.explanation: An appraiser evaluates the value or risk level of the subject matter being insured to help the underwriter set the correct premium.

questions.text: Under the IRDAI (Protection of Policyholders' Interests) Regulations, what is the maximum time allowed to respond to a claim query?
questions.explanation: Insurance companies must raise all requirements or queries regarding a claim at one go and within 30 days of receiving the claim notice.

questions.text: Which of the following is a 'Non-Medical' factor that influences life insurance underwriting?
questions.explanation: Non-medical underwriting factors include occupation (e.g., pilot, miner) and hazardous hobbies (e.g., mountaineering) that increase the risk of death.

questions.text: What does the term 'Orphan Policy' refer to in insurance?
options: A policy taken for a child without parents
A policy where the original agent is no longer active or in service
A policy that has no nominee
A policy that has been lapsed for more than 10 years
questions.explanation: An orphan policy is one where the agent who sold the policy has left the company or is no longer available to provide service to the policyholder.

questions.text: If a policy is issued with a 'Lien', what happens if a death claim occurs during the lien period?
options: The full sum assured is paid
Only a part of the sum assured or the premiums paid are returned
The claim is paid with a 50% penalty
questions.explanation: A lien is a restriction. If death occurs during this period, the insurer pays only a reduced benefit, often to protect against anti-selection in high-risk cases.

questions.text: What is 'Equitable Premium' in life insurance?
options: A premium that is fair and based on the specific risk level of the individual
A premium set by the government
The lowest premium available in the market
questions.explanation: Equity in insurance means that people with similar risks should pay similar premiums, ensuring fairness among all policyholders.

questions.text: Under Section 38 of the Insurance Act, an assignment of a policy can be challenged by the insurer on which grounds?
options: The assignee is not a relative
If the insurer believes the assignment is for the purpose of trading in insurance or is not in the interest of the policyholder
questions.explanation: Insurers can refuse to record an assignment if they suspect it's for speculative trading or against the policyholder's or public interest.

questions.text: Which type of claim is triggered when a policyholder reaches the end of the policy term while alive?
questions.explanation: A maturity claim is the final payout made when the policy term completes and the insured is still surviving.

questions.text: What is the primary document that forms the basis of the insurance contract?
questions.explanation: The Proposal Form is the legal offer made by the customer. All information in it forms the foundation of the contract under 'Utmost Good Faith'.

questions.text: What is 'Numerical Rating' in underwriting?
options: A method where points are assigned to various risk factors to determine the total risk score
The agent's rank in the company
questions.explanation: Numerical rating is a scientific method where underwriters add or subtract points based on health, occupation, and habits to calculate the final premium.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a 'Material Fact' in a life insurance proposal?
options: A dangerous job like deep-sea diving
questions.explanation: Material facts are those that influence the risk assessment. The city of birth usually doesn't affect mortality risk, whereas health and job do.

questions.text: What happens if an 'Appointee' dies before the policyholder and the nominee is still a minor?
options: The policyholder must appoint a new appointee
The court decides the next appointee
questions.explanation: If the appointee dies, the policyholder must immediately designate a new one to ensure the minor nominee's interests are protected.

questions.text: What is the 'Sum at Risk' for an insurance company?
options: The total premium paid by the customer
The difference between the Sum Assured and the accumulated reserve (or fund value)
questions.explanation: Sum at Risk is the actual financial liability of the insurer if death occurs, after accounting for the money already saved by the policyholder in the policy.

questions.text: Which of the following is an ethical duty of an insurance agent?
options: To maximize commission at any cost
To explain all policy exclusions and conditions clearly to the prospect
To hide the client's smoking habit to get lower premiums
To share commission with the customer
questions.explanation: Ethical behavior involves full transparency, ensuring the customer understands what is NOT covered just as much as what IS covered.

questions.text: In a ULIP, who decides the 'Asset Allocation' (where the money is invested)?
questions.explanation: In a Unit Linked Insurance Plan, the policyholder has the freedom to choose and switch between different funds (equity, debt, balanced).

questions.text: What is 'Reinstatement' in life insurance?
options: Reviving a lapsed policy by paying overdue premiums and meeting health requirements
questions.explanation: Reinstatement (Revival) allows a policyholder to bring a lapsed policy back to life within a specific period after the grace period ends.

questions.text: Which rider provides for the payment of a lump sum if the insured becomes totally and permanently unable to work?
questions.explanation: This rider triggers a payout if the insured suffers a disability that permanently prevents them from earning an income.

questions.text: What does Section 41 of the Insurance Act 1938 specifically prohibit?
questions.explanation: Section 41 makes it illegal to offer any rebate or part of the commission to a prospect as an inducement to buy insurance.

questions.text: What is 'Moral Hazard' in financial underwriting?
options: The risk of a person being overweight
The risk when the requested insurance cover is much higher than the person's actual income
The risk of a job loss
questions.explanation: If someone asks for a sum assured that is out of proportion to their earnings, it raises a 'Moral Hazard' as there might be a motive for fraud.

questions.text: Which of the following describes 'Group Insurance' underwriting?
options: Each individual in the group is medically examined
The group as a whole is assessed based on age and occupation, often with limited medicals
Only the group leader is insured
It is same as individual underwriting
questions.explanation: Group underwriting focuses on the characteristics of the group (e.g., all employees of a software firm) rather than checking every single person individually.

questions.text: What is the 'Bonus' in a participating life insurance policy?
options: A share in the surplus profits of the insurer distributed to the policyholder
A discount on the first premium
questions.explanation: Bonuses are the extra amounts added to a 'With-Profit' policy from the company's valuation surplus.

questions.text: Under the MWP Act, can the husband (policyholder) ever change the beneficiaries named in the policy?
options: No, once created, the trust is irrevocable and beneficiaries cannot be changed
Yes, if he pays a fee
questions.explanation: A policy under the Married Women's Property Act creates a trust. It is irrevocable, meaning the policyholder loses control over the policy for the benefit of the wife/children.

questions.text: What is the 'Policy Bond'?
options: A form to be filled for claims
The legal document that serves as evidence of the contract and contains all terms
questions.explanation: The Policy Bond (or Document) is the actual contract that contains the schedule, benefits, and conditions of the insurance.

questions.text: What happens if a policyholder dies after the policy has lapsed but within the grace period?
options: The full sum assured is paid after deducting the unpaid premium
Only the premiums paid are returned
questions.explanation: The policy is still considered active during the grace period. The insurer will pay the death claim but will deduct the pending premium amount.

questions.text: Who is the 'Nominee' in a life insurance policy?
options: The person who pays the premium
The person authorized to receive the claim amount in case of the policyholder's death
The agent who sold the policy
The person who signs the proposal form
questions.explanation: A nominee is a person designated to receive the benefits but does not necessarily own the money (they act as a receiver for legal heirs unless specified).

questions.text: What is 'Term Insurance with Return of Premium' (TROP)?
options: A plan that only pays on death
A term plan that refunds all total premiums paid if the insured survives the term
A plan that pays double on death
questions.explanation: TROP is a variant of term insurance that provides life cover and returns the paid premiums if the person survives till maturity.

questions.text: In insurance, what is a 'Hazardous Occupation'?
options: Working in a chemical factory or underground mining
questions.explanation: Jobs that involve a higher risk of accidents or health issues (like mining or high-voltage electricity) are considered hazardous for underwriting.

questions.text: What does 'Conflict of Interest' for an agent mean?
options: Selling two policies to the same person
Recommending a product that gives more commission to the agent but is not suitable for the customer
Working for two different companies at the same time
questions.explanation: Conflict of interest occurs when an agent's personal gain (commission) clashes with their duty to the customer.

questions.text: What is the 'Survival Period' in a Critical Illness rider?
options: The time the policy has been active
The number of days (usually 15-30) the insured must live after being diagnosed with the illness to get the payout
questions.explanation: Most critical illness riders require the insured to survive for a specific period (e.g., 30 days) post-diagnosis to receive the lump sum.

questions.text: Which document is needed to prove the death was accidental for an Accidental Death Benefit claim?
options: First Information Report (FIR) and Post-Mortem Report
questions.explanation: Legal documents like the FIR and Post-Mortem report are mandatory to verify that the death was indeed caused by an external, violent, and visible accident.

questions.text: What is 'Presumption of Death' after 7 years called under law?
questions.explanation: If someone is missing for 7 years without any news, they are legally presumed dead, allowing the nominee to file a claim.

questions.text: What is the 'Lock-in Period' in a ULIP?
questions.explanation: IRDAI mandates a 5-year lock-in period for all Unit Linked Insurance Plans, during which no partial or full withdrawals are allowed.

questions.text: What is 'Adverse Selection'?
options: High-risk individuals being more likely to apply for insurance than healthy ones
The customer selecting a wrong policy
The agent selecting a wrong customer
questions.explanation: Adverse selection is when people with higher-than-average risks try to get insurance at standard rates without disclosing their risk.

questions.text: Which concept allows an insurer to share the risk with another insurance company?
questions.explanation: Reinsurance is when the primary insurer transfers a portion of the risk to a secondary 'Reinsurer' to manage large liability exposures.

questions.text: What is a 'Level Term' insurance policy?
options: The premium stays the same but the sum assured increases
The sum assured stays constant throughout the policy term
The policy has no maturity benefit
questions.explanation: In a level term plan, the sum assured (death benefit) remains the same from the start of the policy till the end.

questions.text: What is 'Surrender Value'?
options: The amount paid if the insured dies
The cash amount the insurer pays if the policyholder terminates the policy before it matures
questions.explanation: Surrender value is the 'savings' part of a traditional policy that the customer gets back if they decide to quit the policy early.

questions.text: What is an 'Endowment' policy?
options: A combination of life insurance cover and a savings plan
A plan only for old people
questions.explanation: Endowment plans pay the sum assured either on death during the term OR on survival until the end of the term.

questions.text: Which IRDAI system allows customers to track the status of their complaints online?
questions.explanation: IGMS is the central portal by IRDAI where all grievances can be registered and tracked transparently.

questions.text: What is 'Standard Age Proof'?
options: School Leaving Certificate or Birth Certificate
questions.explanation: Documents issued by official authorities like the Municipality or School Boards are considered standard age proofs.

questions.text: Who is the 'Proposer'?
options: The person who sold the policy
The person who applies for the insurance policy
questions.explanation: The proposer is the individual who fills the proposal form. They can be the life assured or someone with an insurable interest in the life assured.

questions.text: What is 'Grace Period' for monthly premium payments?
questions.explanation: For monthly mode policies, the grace period is 15 days. For other modes (Quarterly/Half-yearly/Yearly), it is 30 days.

questions.text: Under Section 39, a nominee is basically a:
options: Trustee or Receiver of the money for legal heirs
The person who pays the premium
questions.explanation: A nominee is a receiver. Unless they are a 'Beneficial Nominee' (like spouse/children in some contexts), they must distribute the money to all legal heirs.

questions.text: What is the 'Premium' in an insurance contract?
options: The benefit given by the company
The consideration paid by the policyholder for the risk cover
questions.explanation: Premium is the price the customer pays to the insurance company in exchange for the promise of financial protection.

questions.text: What is a 'Whole Life' policy?
options: A policy that lasts for 10 years
A policy that provides life cover until the person dies (often defined as up to age 100)
A policy with no death benefit
questions.explanation: Whole life insurance provides lifetime protection. The maturity happens usually at age 100 if the person survives.

questions.text: What is 'Keyman Insurance' used for?
options: To protect the family of the agent
To protect a business from financial loss due to the death of a key employee
To provide insurance to the locksmith
questions.explanation: Keyman insurance is taken by a company on the life of an employee whose skills are vital to the business's success.

questions.text: What is 'Lapse' in insurance?
options: Termination of the policy due to non-payment of premiums within the grace period
questions.explanation: When a policy lapses, the risk cover stops and the policyholder loses the benefits unless the policy has acquired a paid-up value.

questions.text: Which authority can pass an award that is binding on the insurance company?
questions.explanation: If a customer accepts the Ombudsman's decision, the insurance company is legally bound to comply with the award within 30 days.

questions.text: What is the 'Inception Date'?
options: The date the insurance cover officially begins
The date of the agent's birthday
The date the proposal was rejected
questions.explanation: Inception date is the exact date from which the insurance risk is accepted and the coverage starts.

questions.text: What is 'AML' in insurance?
questions.explanation: AML refers to the laws and regulations that prevent people from hiding the origin of illegally obtained money through insurance products.

questions.text: What is 'Indemnity'?
options: A death benefit in life insurance
The principle of providing compensation for the actual loss suffered (common in general/health insurance)
questions.explanation: Indemnity ensures you are not making a profit from a loss; you are just being put back in the same financial state as before the loss.
